design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling levels as RoboCop, eliminate enemies, rescue hostages, advance through story-driven missions
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Action-oriented combat against corporate forces attempting to displace Detroit citizens; balancing offensive firepower with defensive positioning
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as a militarized cyborg law enforcement officer with superhuman capabilities, wielding advanced weaponry to combat corporate corruption
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: SNES version commits to linear side-scrolling action platformer format with shooting mechanics; diverges significantly from the multi-gameplay-style Amiga version, prioritizing arcade-action execution over mechanical variety

progression.unlock_mechanism: Level completion gates access to subsequent stages
replayability_hooks: Multiple weapon types available per stage

world_building_techniques: Story context delivered through news broadcasts between levels (Amiga version documented; likely present in console ports)
Film license adaptation provides pre-established narrative framework about corporate conspiracy in Detroit
Mission-based structure tied to overarching antagonist plot

danger_signals: Corporate-backed forces implementing forced relocation agenda
Cyber-enhanced enemies and robot ninja opponents
Environmental hazards within urban Detroit setting
environmental_storytelling: Detroit urban environment threatened by corporate expansion
Delta City development as visual representation of gentrification/displacement
RoboCop's weaponry and mechanical design as narrative of militarized law enforcement
